identified_property: severity of debt terms and consequences

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: You owe $50,000 to a dangerous loan shark who charges 50% compounding monthly interest, and who has promised severe physical harm to you and your family if you miss even a single payment.

variations.label: low
variations.text: You owe $50,000 at 29% annual interest with a court judgment already entered against you, authorizing immediate wage garnishment and seizure of your assets if you fall behind on the aggressive 12-month repayment schedule.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: You owe $50,000 at 18% annual interest with a strict 2-year repayment deadline, and late payments will trigger significant penalties and a negative mark on your credit report.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: You owe $50,000 at a low 4% annual interest rate, with a comfortable 10-year repayment plan and no penalties for occasionally making late payments.

variations.label: high
variations.text: You owe $50,000 with zero interest and no fixed repayment deadline—the lender has told you to pay it back whenever you're able, at whatever pace works for you.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: You owe $50,000 interest-free to a generous family friend who has told you there's no rush, and who has offered to forgive the remaining balance if you can repay even half over the next several years.

quality_checks.reasoning: All seven tiers describe the same core scenario—owing $50,000—while varying only the severity of the debt terms and consequences. Tier 1 features violent enforcement and predatory compounding interest, making it the worst outcome. Each subsequent tier reduces the severity: from legal judgments and asset seizure (tier 2), to high interest with strict deadlines (tier 3), to the neutral original (tier 4), to favorable low-interest terms (tier 5), to zero interest with no deadline (tier 6), to a largely forgivable debt (tier 7).

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because owing $50,000 to a forgiving lender with no interest and the possibility of partial debt forgiveness represents the mildest possible debt terms and consequences, making it the outcome any rational agent would most prefer.
